And I think I also want to recognize Amanda Brideling, who is sitting there next to our chairman, Small, who will help us out if we have any other specific questions. But in August, in our legislative finance committee hearings, it was forecasted that through corporate income tax, we would expect to have revenues of new money in the area of about $400 million. We were expected to raise $400 million before house resolution number one was passed. Then it was passed, and that changed the forecast dramatically. It changed it to where it was once $400 million raised for the benefit of New Mexicans to then downwards to just barely over $100 million. Why such a decrease? It's because, as I made mentioned before, the passage of federal house resolution number one was to benefit large businesses, multinational corporations, and automatically impact the citizens and the people of New Mexico. That was again. Primarily due to those corporate tax changes again at that federal level. What Senate Bill 151 does is that it actually un-links New Mexico from the two costliest federal changes and links to one federal policy governing the taxation of foreign corporate income. Let me repeat that again. This bill un-links New Mexico from the two costliest federal changes and links to one federal policy that governs the taxation of foreign corporate income. In all 50 states, they have selectively decoupled from portions of the federal tax code. This is not a novel concept that we are trying to do in New Mexico. This doesn't set us apart. We're not an outlier. In all 50 states, they have selectively decoupled themselves from portions of the federal tax code. The decoupling provisions in this bill are particularly popular amongst the states for decoupling because they are so costly to each state's bottom line. New Mexico is not any different than those other states. Let me be clear though, this bill does not decouple every provision. It leaves in place some targeted tax policies like providing incentives for research and development. Companies and small businesses. It leaves those in place. The three areas by which this bill decouples from the federal tax code are these. First, first year bonus depreciation. New Mexico will continue with accelerated depreciations that existed once again before House Resolution No. 1. We've had this and the Mexico was electing to continue to have it and not given to the pressures of an administration federally. Secondly, we will elect to, in our first year, first year expensing for manufacturing facilities. Again, continuing with the previous tax structure that we've enjoyed in New Mexico. Lastly, expanded business deductions for interest. Once again, returning to the status quo that was enjoyed in New Mexico. Once again, for the people in the back, this is not a new tax on New Mexicans. This is not an increase in tax for New Mexicans. I know on social media it may be popular to talk about Senate Bill 151 is coming after you or they're going to raise your costs of living or they're going to do something it really doesn't. This is the same structure that we've enjoyed for decades and through the passage of House Resolution No. 1 on the federal level. It's going to cost our state trillions of dollars by which we are not willing to and have no interest in wanting to share those and allow those corporate profits just to go to the pockets of those billionaires outside of New Mexico. We want to enjoy those benefits here in New Mexico. At this point, it should also be mentioned that, and I've made mention to it before, New Mexico is not an outlier in decoupling. We are not. As a matter of fact, it should be made mention that there are 26 states in the United States that have already decoupled and chosen to decouple from the first year bonus depreciation and many other states across our country are further considering decoupling. Like we are here maintaining accelerated depreciation. As a matter of fact, if we are successful today, we may be the 27th. And a little bit closer to home to give you a little bit of context, one of those 26 today is our neighbor to the west, the state of Arizona. So if the argument wants to be made that we are taking ourselves out of contention to be able to attract new businesses or retain businesses that exist here in New Mexico, there could be nothing farther from the fact that as a matter of fact, we are simply just protecting ourselves our interests and our citizens. Furthermore, 11 states in our nation are decoupling from first year expenses for manufacturing. 12 states in our nation are decoupled from the additional expansion of business deductions. And lastly, this bill, with all it does, it conforms with the federal government's attempt to sweep in all of the monies from the United States companies that have established foreign subsidiaries overseas and pushed assets and cash to lower tax areas, that's been a problem for the federal government for decades. Let's stash our money overseas and somewhere in some place that we don't know where it's at. We are conforming with the latest efforts to add those back to our tax base, as well as already 22 other states in our nation currently do. This, again, ladies and gentlemen, Mr. Speaker, this vehicle continues the status quo that we have enjoyed for decades. This continues the status quo that we have enjoyed for decades. It pushes back on wanting to put the dollars back into corporate profits and it maintains that the dollars that are raised in New Mexico stay in New Mexico. As a matter of fact, the vehicle in Senate Bill 151 raises more than $110 million once fully implemented. And future proofs us, excuse me, from future changes in these sections unless we agree to them in New Mexico. That's Senate Bill 151 and the vehicle by which we are going to raise money to ensure that we can have the capacity to enjoy these additions and these incentives that the Senate brought over into our attention in our committee this morning. Again, Senate Bill 151 includes those provisions by which they elected to include as tax incentives into this tax omnimous bill. I will go over those details right now. In sections 1 and 2, excuse me, yep, in sections 1 and 2, it covers, again, those things that I mentioned earlier in my presentation. It decouples us from those federal tax policies. Section 3 and 4, what they elected to do in the Senate and send over our way is the appreciation for what is known as the local journalist refundable credit, local news wages, that is Senate Bill 120. What that does, it creates a refundable tax credit for local news organizations equal to 30% of annual wages. Furthermore, it's designed to support local journalism capacity by offsetting payroll costs and incentivizing the employment or retention of qualified journalists. Furthermore, the statewide annual cap of $4 million total credit per year is attached to this part of their tax package. Which it would apply beginning in 2027 and sunset in 2032. The next portion that the Senate sent over as a part of its tax package to provide incentives or tax credits or tax incentives to the Mexicans is 1 in section 5. And that is titled the Affordable Manufacturing Multi-Family Housing Construction GRT deduction. That is under Senate Bill 92. What Senate Bill 92 does is it creates a GRT deduction for the construction materials and labor used in affordable multi-family housing projects. The intention of this bill is to reduce project costs for qualifying affordable multi-family developments, improving viability to leverage public and private housing resources. This again will go effective and go live in July of 2027 with the sunset of 2030. Next, what the Senate sent over for its inclusion into the tax packages here is section 6. Section 6 includes physician income tax credit, Senate Bill 12. What Senate Bill 12 does is that it creates a $10,000 personal income tax credit for qualified physicians. The criteria being that those physicians must be licensed in New Mexico, providing at least 1,584 hours of health care service to our state for our citizens per year. And eligibility is certified by the Department of Health. This, in fact, ladies and gentlemen and Mr. Speaker, will be the biggest physician personal income tax incentive in the country. We talked a lot this year, Mr. Speaker, and members of this body about how do we fix the doctor's shortage in New Mexico? How do we fix health care in New Mexico? There have been a number of provisions that have passed out of this house, and I think that we are all proud of the work that all of us have done in the very bipartisan nature. This adds to that collective. This adds to that collective that unlike complicated GRT deductions that have impacts on local government, this credit for the doctors will offset GRT. Payed by physicians with no local governmental impact at all. Simple and easy to apply for. This would go into effect in January of 2027, and with sunset in January of 2032. What was also added in the Senate provisions to their tax package was Section 7. Noted as the Health Care Equipment GRT deduction, Senate Bill 133. What Senate Bill 133 does, or was supposed to do, and I'm reading you on to something that we did, is that it was supposed to create a new deduction on selling certain in-office equipment and non-prescription in-office medications to health care providers. Now, in our committee, as a part of our tax package, we struck that provision. We struck that provision for one purpose that was pretty well talked about in many circumstances, and that was the effect that that GRT would have on our local municipalities. We heard loud and clear from those local governments, and we were not willing to put them in a compromising position for this bill. So it takes them out of the risk of losing that GRT capacity that they enjoyed today. Finally, in Section 7 and 8, the Senate sent over this provision called the local news printer credit, Senate Bill 150. What Senate Bill 150 does is it creates a refundable tax credit for businesses that provides for manufacturing, production, and printing services for a local news organization. It's a credit of up to $10,000 for each employee employed by the news printer. And just as the others have had a sunset, this one does, too. It will begin in 2027, and it will sunset in 2032. That is the Senate's tax package. That's the vehicle by which the capacity is raised and those provisions by which they are electing and proposing the capacity is spent on to help New Mexicans all across our state. Coming now to the work that our House Taxation and Revenue Committee did this past session. We heard numerous bills as we all do in our respective committees, all with great merit, all with great incentives, all with fantastic ideas. And for those that were not selected to be in this year's tax package, I would encourage those sponsors to come back. Because those ideas that you have, those people that you represented are very much worth it. We want to see them back because we believe that they had absolutely excellent merit. However, we are bound by a certain dollar capacity. And in being responsible, we cannot go over that dollar capacity. So now knowing that in the House Taxation and Revenue Committee, we have about $60 million to build our side of the tax package. What do you do with $60 million where you can create good tax policy, where you can do something that's good for everybody, where you can do something that you can hang your hat on and feel like you've done something great for the people of New Mexico. Well, what we did this year for the House Amendments to the Senate Tax Package is in Section 9. What Section 9 does and what it's titled is the high wage job tax credits that is under House Bill 145. And I want to recognize one of our minority committee members, the gentleman from Sandeval County, Representative Hernandez, that brought this to our attention. What the high wage job tax credit does is it extends the sunset for the high wage job tax credit from something that's going to expire July 1 of this very year. And it extends it all the way to July 1 of 2036. At this point, what this high wage job tax credit does is that employers that create high wage jobs in New Mexico may apply for a tax credit against state GRT. Compensating tax, withholding tax, the interstate telecommunications tax, GRT, E911 surcharges, and telecommunication relay services surcharges. And as an eligible employer, the employer is eligible for JTIP administered by the Economic Development Department. And so at this point in time, the total cost that we would expand from the House's side of the capacity is between $5 and $6 million of the 60 that I referenced earlier. Now where this gets a little bit different, I will say it in that way, is that in this body, when we don't have enough capacity in our committee, we don't have enough capacity to do something groundbreaking. Something that we can say that gosh, we are changing the landscape for tax policy in our state because those are big spends. Those things often cost $100 million plus if we intend to work the way that it should work. So if you recall in this body, in this chamber about two weeks ago, we heard a bill called House Bill 2. In House Bill 2, there was a provision in there that afforded a 1% compensation increase for all of our public employees. That was something that the Appropriations Committee voted on proudly in a bipartisan manner as I understand it. That was something that this body stood behind in full force in a bipartisan supportive manner that we passed. We passed in this House, sent it over to the Senate, and it's not our place to question the rationale, but for some reason that 1% compensation increase was removed. It was removed. And when it's removed and our people are left out in the cold with their pockets not as full as they could be, we have to do something to make it right. So instead of trying to scramble to create a tax package with a bunch of Christmas tree or Christmas ornaments to decorate that tree and try to make something out of nothing, what our committee elected to do, what our staff was able to help us collaborate with, was that we thought of, well, why don't we take care of the people? Why don't we take care of the people of New Mexico? Why don't we take care of those public employees? Let's take care of those school teachers. Let's take care of those university employees. Let's take care of the janners and the people that work in this very building. Let's take care of those police officers and those firefighters and everyone else that may be a public employee in this state. And let's do what is the right thing to do, not politically motivated, not pushed by somebody else, but sometimes it's okay in this building to do something that doesn't have to score political points. And that's what this bill does. That's what our provision to reinstate the 1% compensation back into this legislature for consideration by the governor does is that it uses the capacity, allowed it to the house in our tax package to fully fund a 1% salary increase for public employees that include, again, state agencies, higher education institutions and public schools. This matches the compensation plan, again, as I made mentioned before, that was included in the house past budget before, again, it was unfortunately stripped by the Senate. We're going to make it right. In addition to that, I made mention of House Bill 145, the Highway's Jobs Tax Credit. I also made mention, again, of what we did to strip out the GRT deduction on medical equipment and supplies and response, again, to the concerns that we heard from local governments on the impacts to their revenues. What we also did is that we arranged dates. This was a provision that came over from the Senate that postponed the House's ability to enact its spend until 2028, so they pushed this out a year. But because of the urgency for these 1% compensation raises to become law, because of the urgency that this Highway's Jobs Tax Credit is, because it expires on July 1st of this very year, we reversed that. So we now push that, and we amended in the bill, that the House's spend begin this next fiscal year in FY2027, and that the Senate is pushed back to FY2028. And finally, Mr. Speaker and general men and general lady of the House, what our amendments do is that it ensures that sunset dates passed by the Senate were kept and it also adds sunsets to sections where they previously did not exist in keeping with best practices for evaluating tax expenditures. And so with all that said, Mr. Speaker, we believe in our committee that we have done the right thing in our amendment. We can argue about the merits of, well, we should raise the money differently, or maybe we have money in the bank or some other thing. But at the end of the day, I'll go back to the first thing I said when I started this presentation. The vehicle used in Senate Bill 151. It is not a new tax. It's not an expansion of a tax. It is the status quo of what was enjoyed prior to July of 2025 when the federal government elected to pass House Resolution No.1. We are using our sovereign status as a state. We are selecting to maintain that we are going to protect the Mexico and the Mexicans the right way. We are simply doing what other states have already done. We may, if this becomes law, become the 27th state in the nation to do this. And for those of us that are here that are concerned that it takes us out of the running to attract new businesses or retain those that are currently here, it doesn't. Because at this point, New Mexico is a great place to live. We should all be proud to represent New Mexico. And with that, Mr. Speaker, I will stand for any questions. There is a debate. Yes, we have social programs, Mr. Speaker and General Lady, that will help those that may not be able to work. That's not what we're talking about here. You grow up on these dusty roads, and you're born and raised, and you cut your teeth, and you cut your legs, and you play with your friends, and you grow up there knowing that it's a sense of home. That's your community, right? You represent, in my case, I represent San Diego Pueblo. And in doing so, you know for a fact, Mr. Speaker in general, that I could have gone anywhere for the university, but I elected to want to stay home, because it was near and dear to me. And probably, too, I can get anywhere else as a student. But electing to also not just do that, but Mr. Speaker in general, making sure that I want to raise my family in the place that I grew up in hopes that they can appreciate the type of lifestyle and livelihood that I experienced when I grew up. That's what I want from my family, I think that's what anybody in this body or anybody in the state would want for their own children as well. And as you grow older, and as I grow older, I wanted that for my grandkids, and I think we all want the same and the same for the unborn, but knowing that in that cohesion, in that collaboration, in that family, in that home life that you talk about, and if journalism and journalist as a profession is what ties that in because you want to report on the goodness or the problems that happen in your respective communities. That's the beauty of what I believe that the Senate wanted to include in this package, and that's why we appreciate what they've done. They did last year, it was vetoed out, they did it this year, and I think it's appreciated just the same way it was last year. And so Mr. Speaker in general, when we talk about things like this, it's not done because Mr. Speaker in general, we're trying to create winners and losers, or maybe the journalists have a better lobbyist than anybody else does. That's not the case here. The case here, Mr. Speaker general, it is that this is absolutely worthwhile, this provides the cohesion that we talk about, and this provides the lifestyle that many people long for, and for those of us in New Mexico, we are lucky to still have and retain. Thank you, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen. I want to talk a little bit now because you mentioned the Senate and their intention with the package that they brought over to the House this morning. But you also talked about a veto that we faced last year, and when you talk about the Senate, how did the Senate respond to our amendments and to our additions to the tax package? Mr. Speaker, gentlemen. Mr. Speaker and gentle lady, are you talking about this year? And the bill we're talking about today? Mr. Speaker, gentlemen, yes, when we spoke with the chair of Senate tax, how is it received in this morning? That's the funny aspect about being in a committee like tax, in terms of like, we work on our own things in the Senate and in the House, and then we come together to create a larger tax omnibus package right in the chair from the Senate tax and the chair from House tax. It's shoulder to shoulder presenting a bill, right? And in this case, Senate brought over a bill, Mr. Speaker and general lady that had what they believed was the best tax package that they could provide to us. And then shoulder to shoulder seen next to her, the Senate tax share. Mr. Speaker, general lady, we say, but we have an amendment and we're going to strike a couple of things and we're going to change the way the funding distribution takes place, but we're going to add these things. Mr. Speaker, general lady, I can't officially comment on what they're going to do. Hopefully, if this passes this body today, but I can say, Mr. Speaker and gentle lady, that I would say the reception of the Senate tax share was friendly, was cordial, was appreciative, especially in regards to the 1% compensation that was put back into this bill. Thank you, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen. And when we think about the priorities that Senate Bill 151 has, and when I think about the executive and the vision and the priorities that they put out, I think that they're pretty aligned. So when I think about health care, when I look at back at our special sessions where we strengthen and support a journalism and radio stations, especially in tribal areas, that this tax package is also pretty aligned with not only the Senate, what we heard this morning, but the executive, can you speak to that a little bit, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen? Mr. Speaker and general lady, thank you for bringing that up. I think that's a great point to add on is that this is not a knee jerk reaction to anything that has taken place since we've entered 2026. Mr. Speaker and general lady, I think this is a continuation of the work that we know that as legislators are either issues that plague New Mexico or the prospects that we can make New Mexico a real big time player on a national or even a global scene, right? So Mr. Speaker, general lady, you talked about the special sessions that we engaged in here in the latter part of maybe the half and latter part of 2025, you know, the time that we spend here as a body when the governor calls us back for special session, we want to make sure that we don't, we don't just take those an excuse just to come back to Santa Fe, that's never the intent, Mr. Speaker, general lady, when we call for special session, it's done because there's an identified problem and that we want to have a solution to fix it. Mr. Speaker, general lady, you identified several of those that we've done over the past year at this point and so this is absolutely without a doubt my mind, Mr. Speaker and general lady, a continuation of the great work that all of us in this body, maybe I'm being overzealous and saying that the great work, but we should all be able to pat ourselves in the back because we are responding to certain situations and circumstances that are affecting New Mexico today. And so whether it's in a special session or whether it's in a 30-day session and for those of us, Mr. Speaker and general lady that are actually looking forward to the 60-day session, this is a continuation of that, right? We want to make sure that we are not taking our eye off the target that we are being responsive, that we don't want to be reactive, but instead we should be proactive in how we are doing things in New Mexico. Thank you, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen, and if I can build off of that as well, what I've seen as you said is a continuation of a conversation we've been having for 12, 15 months and that there have been both priorities in this house in the Senate and with the executive that focus on housing in particular around affordable housing. This is the time where the lowest amount of 35 and under have owned a home. And so this is historical where we've seen the lowest percentage of homeownership in the country and so as a state, we've really prioritized putting funding, policy and land acquisition towards affordable housing. And so I think that that's really wise and strategic of us and that we're continuing that conversation. In addition, the latest jobs report also showed that health care was one of the industries that is driving the job growth in the country. And so we want to take advantage of that and we want to say that we are ready for health care providers to come to come to New Mexico to stay in New Mexico and that there's all of these benefits that they can get. And that this is a way that they can practice in a place in an environment that is more friendly to health care professionals. And so, again, taking advantage of the economic currents and saying, look, health care is on the rise. Jobs here are growing. How do we grow that in New Mexico and take advantage of that windfall. And then second with our schools and with journalism as we've seen on the national level, these huge newspapers getting taken over by billionaires, we want to really invest in local voices and local journalism. And again, we've seen that across the House, the Senate and the executive. And so I really appreciate that, again, this isn't, as you said, a knee jerk reaction, but it is a continuation of our ability to plan strategically and have strategic intent in the midst of some pretty huge federal economic gyrations. And so speaking of that, I want to move a little bit to talk about the economics, where we're getting this capacity in the first place. So I don't know if there's anything else you want to say before I move the conversation into the decoupling and the corporate income tax, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen. Mr. Speaker and General Lady as much as I like to continue to talk about where we come from and places we call home and continue to brag upon the things that we believe that we are doing proactively to help in the Mexico. I think I'm okay with transitioning to talking about the vehicle by which we're trying to pay for all this. Thank you, Mr. Speaker and gentlemen. So I'm wondering, first, do states have the right to make choices about their tax policies in the United States, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen? Mr. Speaker and General Lady absolutely. Thank you, Mr. Speaker and gentlemen. And then when the government makes its own choices about its tax policy that impact us, we then as a state are obligated to then look at the economic conditions and plan accordingly. Would you would you agree with that statement, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen? Mr. Speaker and General Lady, absolutely. And really as we talk about our ability to do things differently or independently as a state, right? It's got to obviously align with our values as New Mexicans and as New Mexico and also align with our economic conditions in our state. Thank you, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen. And what was your reaction when you got the news that our revenue from the corporate income tax because we are, we conform with the federal tax rate that it went from 400 million to 100 million? What was your reaction to that, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen? Mr. Speaker and General Lady, I often watch the reaction from our esteemed appropriations, share the gentlemen from Donia Anna before I react to myself. But upon my initial reaction, I was stunned and appalled and felt really unfortunate for the people in New Mexico knowing that that extra capacity in this case going from 400 million to just a little over 100 million was going to be taken away from the people in New Mexico and the things that we could utilize that for. In this case, those 1% wage increases was really unfortunate knowing that it was going to go to those large multi national corporations for their own likely benefit and profit. Thank you, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen. And I don't know if you or your expert might know about this, but that wasn't the only economic headwind we were facing as a state. Can you talk a little bit about are there were there other headwinds that we were experiencing as a state that we had to plan for as we were approaching this 30 day session, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen. Mr. Speaker and General Lady, absolutely. So I mean, when you talk about those headwinds, not only was it with HR one and the tax, the tax code discussion, but also oil and gas prices had been, had been falling significantly, right? Hiring had slowed across our country to Mexico, not being an exception. The job market is harder to find a job in any one location and not just in our state, but all across our country and Mr. Speaker and General Lady. Golly inflation inflation is eating at wages that aren't growing quickly enough to help suffice that. Thank you, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen. So again, back to that 1% cost of living adjustment that our New Mexicans, not only, you know, families and individuals, but they are seeing their own economic headwinds as we are facing a state that our families are also facing those same headwinds. So when we look at that that cut from 400 million to a little, a little over 100 million, where we consulted was the state consulted by our federal government about this shift as they were debating HR one, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen. Mr. Speaker and and General Lady, no, we were not. So Mr. Speaker, gentlemen, when we are not consulted and the government, the federal government makes all of these economic changes very quickly, very rapidly. We look at tariffs. We look at I would even say the decrease in in migration that impacts economic and workforce and our workforce in New Mexico that all of those things compounded. So where does that put us as a state? I know that there's a number of states in our country that are now facing recession like environment. What's it like here in New Mexico? Are we in a recession? Are we teetering? Are we robust and resilient? What's the? Senator, Mr. Speaker? State your point. Mr. Speaker, I don't think that this conversation has anything to do other states, what they've done with recession, language like that. We need to stick to the bill. Thank you, General Lady. We'll stick to the bill. Please continue. Mr. Speaker, gentlemen, so can you talk a little bit about what New Mexico is facing in terms of its own economic headwinds? Are we resilient? Are we trading water? Mr. Speaker, gentlemen. Mr. Speaker and General, that's a great question and I also want to appreciate the comment by our minority leader to be focused on this. I appreciate that. Regarding your question, General Lady, from Donia and I County, no state is recession-proof, right? But luckily enough for New Mexico, Mr. Speaker and General Lady, that over the past four to six years I imagine that this body, this legislature, with really being led by the professionals in the legislative finance committee, have helped us to create a sense of stability that once was this feast or famine mode that we have been trapped in for years, right? And so today, to your point, Mr. Speaker and General Lady, as it respects to your question, New Mexico today is in a good financial position, especially compared with other states. But we are really, Mr. Speaker and General Lady, what we would say, we're on a nice edge if we don't keep up with our economic momentum. That we could risk worsening revenues if in fact a recession takes place. And so in this case, some of the Mexicans may feel like they are in a recessionary environment today with wages not keeping up. But in terms of our government as a whole, I think that the things that we have done in regards to building the capacity to create trust funds, to create mechanisms that we cannot have to be at the will and the whim of oil and gas prices. To dictate whether or not departments or programs continue or that capital outlay continue that we are on a trajectory to be able to sustain and withstand a recession should what happened. Thank you, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen. I would also credit the work that the House appropriations and tax and rev and other committees and elected leaders here have done. And the majority have done to create the conditions for us to be more resilient in times of economic headwinds and potential chaos that we're seeing. And so the funds that we've created, but also as you said, we hold that additional money from severance tax bonds and our severance tax permanent fund. So we're not spending all of our cash and we maintain about 30% reserves. Is that correct, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen? Mr. Speaker and General, the absolutely and that's something that as a body, we should all celebrate that we are physically responsible in that way and that all the revenues that we receive in the state aren't just automatically for no reason at all just spent because we have the dollars at hand. So thank you, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen. And so we have demonstrated a lot of creativity over the last several years in creating these conditions. And I would argue that we've also seen a lot of creativity from the federal government as they change their tax policies and rules to benefit a certain group of people in population. And so would you say that this times like this also require a level of creativity and that this is a proper response given what is happening in our country right now, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen? Mr. Speaker and General, I think it's really just our sovereignty as a state to be able to say we're going to do its best for our state and for our people. Whether you want to call it creative or bold or imaginative, right? We want to make sure that we're saying that it's just the right thing to do. It's the prudent thing to do as physical agents or as agents of our state here as legislatures in this body. And so we talk about our response on a federal level here in New Mexico. I mean, it's through vehicles like this, the decoupling statutes and it's through other vehicles that we've talked about and debated fiercely on this House floor. On other social aspects of life today. And so when we talk about the fiscal impacts of what's going on on a global level, on a national level, how it relates down to our state level and even how it how it trickles down to those municipalities and local governments, making sure that we protect at a minimum our citizens, those local governments protecting the values of our of our New Mexico government here. And so that we can still become and be and retain ourselves as a player nationally and globally. Thank you, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen. And so thinking about our creativity and looking at how others have also done this in the last year is that we are choosing to decouple our corporate income tax from conforming with the federal tax code. Is that correct, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen? Mr. Speaker and gentlemen, do New Mexicans get to decouple themselves from interest rates or high inflation or higher prices at the grocery store? Mr. Speaker, gentlemen? Mr. Speaker and gentle lady know but imagine the world that that would be if we could. Thank you, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen. I know that's a little cheeky, but I but I say it in all seriousness that our families don't have a choice of going in and saying, hey, I need a lower interest rate or hey, inflation is too much. Can we can we dial it down? Our families don't have that choice. And so for us as legislators and policymakers, it is our obligation for us to be able to pull as many levers as possible to create the conditions for them to succeed. Would you agree with that, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen? Mr. Speaker and gentlemen, not only did I agree, but I overwhelmingly agree and I think of examples like, you know, when we talk about our ability to provide a 1% increase our compensation to our state employees. And I think about you made mention of this example earlier of Chapparel and knowing that the public schools are where a majority of the people that live in Chapparel work, right. And I can see this in so many local and rural communities across our state where for those folks that live in very rural towns. And I'll take probably the world, the most ruralist town that I visited in 2025. And that's probably mascara in New Mexico when we were invited for a legislative finance committee, you know, those folks that work at that school are likely farmers and ranchers. And so they depend on that job. And so a 1% increase is absolutely something they can take, right. But also as farmers and ranchers, Mr. Speaker and gentlemen, they're price takers, right. They're not price makers. And so when you have to be at the will and the whim of global and federal and then state, and you can't choose to decouple, those are realized circumstances that we have to be mindful of. So when we can provide this type of balancing act, if you will, to provide an economic relief, if you will, if you want to call it something else, that we are doing things in a proactive nature to help all of those in our state, especially in this case, those on a more rural level. Thank you, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen. And when we talk about decoupling, and I know I hope it's okay that I do talk about our neighboring states. Because this is, I think, the policy that we're creating does impact our ability to attract businesses to retain businesses and that we're competing with neighboring states. And so when we look at the decoupling and looking at, and I want to look particularly at full expensing versus bonus depreciation, is there research about which one is better for a state? Yeah, is there like empirical evidence that like this way is way better than this other way of doing it? Mr. Speaker, gentlemen. Mr. Speaker and gentlemen, a very interesting question. And so when we think about is one better than the other, there's really not a consensus in research that there are only certain folks on either side that may argue one or the other but ultimately state or state corporate income taxes are not a large share. Of corporate expenditures. And so that would be the answer to your question. Thank you, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen, because I know that the federal government is moving more towards full expensing and that we are sticking closer to bonus depreciation. Is that correct, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen? Mr. Speaker and gentle lady actually know where we're actually sticking with accelerated expenses and so that's that's in our case, the reasoning why the sponsor, at least on the Senate side, put that in this legislation based on the professional recommendations obviously of our economists that help craft this bill. Thank you, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen, and can you explain accelerated depreciation just in the nutshell for me again, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen? Sure, absolutely. So Mr. Speaker and generally accelerated depreciation is when the depreciation is based off of the useful life of a property rather than 100% of the cost of the of the first year. Thank you, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen. So if I can repeat back and correct me if I'm wrong. So so full expenses I can write off as a business owner. I can write off the full expense at the front ends and take that as a deduction. Is that correct, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen? Mr. Speaker and gentle lady, yes, ma'am. Okay, thank you, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen, and then accelerate accelerated depreciation is I am taking a small percentage of the full expense of that expenditure, whether it's a piece of equipment or or machinery. And I can take a percentage of that every year until it loses until it's at zero value, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen. Mr. Speaker and generally you're you're right on. So yes, is the answer to your question with a little bit of explanation and usually with a little bit of a bigger share in the first year like like 40%. Thank you, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen, and and is why would the federal government stay at 100% of full expense? Why is that choice being made, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen? Well, Mr. Speaker and general lady, I think there's a number of thoughts behind this, but really I think the intent is to encourage investment and to encourage spending by increasing the deduction against against one's taxes. Thank you, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen, and but isn't it also true that when we do accelerated depreciation that that there is more money generated locally, isn't there like a local generated benefit when states take on accelerated depreciation. And I'm asking a question that I actually don't know the answer to. I'm really curious as I'm trying to understand this, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen. Thank you, Mr. Speaker and general lady, so I think personally I'm not really familiar with with that take and I can look to our economists here, but certainly we have to consider the full picture with other economic development incentives and taxes that are available at the current time. But in terms of your question specifically, I'm not really familiar with being able to provide you the answer that you don't know either. Thank you, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen, because I'm looking at the FIR for Senate Bill 151, and it does say policy makers should also recognize that state level implications of bonus depreciation differ from federal considerations. And so that there's a different impact. So we can't really compare the federal government policy choice and say we're going to have that same outcome if we do what they're doing. And so that's why we're making a different choice. I think that's what I'm trying to understand, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen. So Mr. Speaker and generally that that's a great point. And so really in terms of context right in providing the context for us. State taxes is just so much smaller than federal taxes, right? And so that point is well taken, but really at the end of the day they still enjoy the benefit of the federal deduction. So our decoupling won't necessarily discourage those types of large investments into our state. Thank you, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen. And at any point in our history has New Mexico ever decoupled from the federal government? Mr. Speaker, gentlemen. Mr. Speaker and generally the yes man we have we've decoupled before even as early as 2019, right? And in 2019 when we had to make adjustments to the tax cuts and jobs act, right? We had to do that back in 2019 just to make sure that we were able to maintain a balance here in our state. Thank you, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen. So this isn't new for us and it's actually fairly recent for us to have decoupled from the federal government. Mr. Speaker and generally to that point, I mean, this is not a novel concept. It's one that's widely accepted, widely utilized, not only just in New Mexico, but all across our country. So I mean, when we talk about decoupling, it's not a bad word, sort of speak, right? It's something that states have the ability and the right to utilize to make sure that they can protect the dollars that are rightfully theirs. Thank you, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen. And this is not permanent either if you talk about we've done it before. So if we decide there's a better economic environment where it would make sense for us to conform again, is that a choice that we would have available to us, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen? Mr. Speaker and General, yes, absolutely. So this doesn't tie our hands into perpetuity, right? I mean, this absolutely just provides us the tools during this certain time in our economic lifestyle currently so that we can make the best choices and do the right things for New Mexico. Thank you, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen. And since 2001, so for the past 25, 26 years, how many times has the federal government adjusted the depreciation percentage, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen, or has it stayed the same? Oh, Mr. Speaker and General, that's a great question. So really, it's happened so many times that this has taken place, right? I think I would refer you to page 9 on our FIR that I think shows the numbers of ups and downs and peaks and valleys that we've seen. And so since 2019 to your point, I mean, it's happened so many times. Thank you, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen. I counted them as 13. So in the last over two decades, the federal government has adjusted its own percentages doing their own analysis of the economic, their own economic analysis of what's ahead and they decided they're different presidents that decided to do different policies. And so, again, this isn't anything new that the federal government's doing, but it is creating an impact on us as a state and that it, again, it is within our right to make a choice given that impact. Is that correct, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen? Mr. Speaker and General, yes, ma'am, that is correct. Thank you, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen. And in terms of decoupling and the corporate expenses, how much of their corporate budget is state taxes? How much, like, what percentage of their total budget are they paying in state taxes, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen? That's a great question. And so at this point, what they're paying in the state taxes really less than a quarter of 1% is to cost corporate expenses. And that is actually from a 2010 study that our LLC reminds me of. Thank you, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen. And I want to go back to, well, I want to go to another piece of the corporate income tax is adding a portion section 951A to the tax base. So can you talk about that? It sounds like what we are trying to avoid is having larger corporations move profits to foreign countries that we cannot tax when they're doing work in New Mexico. Am I getting that right, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen? Mr. Speaker, in general, it is, yes, ma'am. And it's often called, quote unquote, necktie. And the federal government has struggled with this issue time and time again. But it is absolutely an issue in itself. Thank you, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen. And why would a state choose to continue to do that practice? So why would a state continue to allow a corporation that operates in its boundaries in its state to then take revenue into a foreign country and not allow it to be taxed, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen? Mr. Speaker, in general, lady, well, our state or any state, right, may choose to do so, but that really doesn't in New Mexico align with, with our values, right? If you earn in New Mexico, you should be able to pay your fair shake here in New Mexico as well. And so this is simply what we're trying to do and maintain the status quo here for New Mexico. Thank you, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen. And so because we also ask New Mexicans to do that, we ask New Mexicans to pay their fair share in state income tax. I'm a business owner, I pay GRT. And so because I do business in New Mexico and when I do, I pay into our gross receipts tax. And so again, this is creating some cohesion amongst people who are doing business in New Mexico. Do you agree with that, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen? Mr. Speaker, in general, lady, yes. And your analogy to a single state taxpayer is absolutely correct, right? I mean, if you earn here, you should pay here. And I think the same should go for corporate as well. Thank you, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen. And then the other thing I want to note is that there are many other states are making their own choices because of HR1. And a corporation that is doing business in New Mexico, but maybe also doing business in another state or in various states. That business now has to figure out what tax code is being applied in each different state. Is that correct, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen? Mr. Speaker, in general, lady, relative really to their activity and in any certain state or in New Mexico, at least those are state. I'm going to maintain our state in New Mexico. We'll regulate that, but the short answer to your question would be yes. Thank you, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen. That feels pretty jarring for a corporation. And I would make the argument that HR1 actually created a lot of instability for corporations that are working in multiple states because where there was conformity and people could make budgets out from that conformity. Now there is a lot of inconsistency that businesses may have to navigate. And again, I wouldn't want to put that blame on New Mexico because we are simply making a choice based on choices that the federal government made. But HR1 is creating some instability and inconsistency for corporations that are in multi states. Would you agree with that, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen? Mr. Speaker, in general, lady, absolutely. I agree with that. Thank you, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen. And I think the last thing I want to talk about is, let's see, I had one more question. Mr. Speaker, gentlemen, I want to thank you for just your answers and going through pretty diligently the SB151 and talking about the investments that we're making, but also the reasoning behind the tax capacity, where that tax capacity is coming from. And if I could do all states have our all states having to answer this question of do we decouple or do we not or are some states completely doing something different that maybe might be available to us or not available to us, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen. Mr. Speaker, in general, I actually know because some states in some cases were never were never coupled in the first place, right? And so I think at this point that that statement would be wrong. Thank you, Mr. Speaker. Gentlemen, I appreciate that. But I would just think, I would say, and I'll close, is that I think what New Mexico is doing is not as aggressive as what other states are doing in terms of how they're taxing corporations. I think what we're doing is precise and it's also proper, a proper response to what we're seeing. Any state was facing a $300 million shortfall where we were expecting $400 million in tax revenue, and now we're facing a $300 million cut from that. I think that this is, this answer is precise and proper given what we're facing in our state. Given what our families are going to be facing in the future, because those economic headwinds aren't going to go away. And so making sure that we are insulating New Mexicans, our families, but also our institutions as much as possible, is within our right and within all of our freedom to do in our tax policies. And so I want to thank you for, for that creativity and imagination, and also putting New Mexicans at the center of these ideas and these proposals. So with that, I yield. Thank you, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen. There is further debate. The gentleman from St. Juan, Representative Duncan. Thank you, Mr. Speaker. Will the gentleman yield? Thank you, Mr. Speaker. I yield to a ranking member from St. Juan County. Thank you, Mr. Speaker. Mr. Chair, I've got a few questions for you if I could. Mr. Speaker and gentlemen, all during the interim, there was no tax package, no tax package, no tax package. In fact, until up until a couple of weeks ago, maybe even more a week ago, maybe there was no tax package allowed. I think Mr. Speaker and gentlemen, Mr. Chair, that if I heard correctly that this is something that's been talked about for up to a year to 15 months now, is that correct? Mr. Speaker and gentlemen, I don't recall saying that. Mr. Speaker and gentlemen, the previous representative referred to that when she said, yeah, this is something we've been talking about for about 12 to 15 months. So if this tax package was something that's been talked about. Mr. Speaker and gentlemen, my experts have better ears than I do and their memories much sharper than mine, but I think we're talking about Mr. Speaker and gentlemen, the federal decouple. Mr. Speaker and gentlemen, the previous representative referred to that.

The New Mexico House, the House of Representatives of the City of New Mexico recognizes Representative Martín R. Zamora.

Whereas since he was first elected in 2018, Representative Martín R. Zamora has served in four terms, served four terms in the legislature over more than seven years as a Republican state representative for the residents of House District 63.

And whereas Representative Zamora is retiring from the legislature in 2026 to run for a seat in the United States House of Representatives as a candidate for the New Mexico Third Congressional District.
And whereas Representative Zamora was born on a farm near Clovis and he comes from a long line of farmers, ranchers and cattlemen who have called his region home since before statehood.
And whereas over the years, Representative Zamora and his wife Debbie, Debbie who is a Clovis City Counselor, have owned farmland in Curry County and a ranch in Torrance and Sakura counties.

And he has built a successful career in agriculture, cattle and horse trading, the fertilizer business, steel, trucking and property investments while always staying true to the rural values of hard work, faith and family.

Whereas Representative Zamora and his wife Debbie have been married for 46 years and are the proud parents of two daughters who are both registered nurses and grandparents is six wonderful grandchildren.
And whereas Representative Zamora is referred to as Papa, not potato and Spanish but a beloved grandfather and has been a great example and mentor to his grandchildren.

One of his fondest memories was when he brought his grandson for a few days to shadow him at the legislature and his grandson went home and played legislature in his room for days and was heard screaming over and over Mr. Speaker.

And whereas Representative Zamora saying the gambler when he presented his first bill and has been heard saying, you've got to know and to hold him, no one to fold him, no one to walk away and no one to run.

And whereas Representative Zamora has served in the legislature as ranking member and member of the Sanding House Agriculture Asakya and Water Resources Committee and as a member of the Sanding House Government Elections and Indian Affairs Committee.

And whereas Representative Zamora has also served as a member of the Interim Economic Enroll Development and Policy Committee the Interim Indian Affairs Committee and the Interim Water and Natural Resources Committee as an advisory member of the Interim Transportation Infrastructure Revenue Subcommittee and the Interim Economic Enroll Development Committee and as a special advisory member of the Interim Revenue Stabilization and Tax Policy Committee.

And whereas during his more than seven years of service in the legislature, Representative Zamora sponsored and worked to pass legislation on many public issues important to the residents of House District 63 and the people of the state, including legislation in 2024 and 2025 that authorized the New Mexico Finance Authority to make loans or grants from the state's water project fund for water projects across the state.
And whereas Representative Zamora has been a strong advocate for farmers and ranchers and he regularly sponsors and works to pass legislation to support agriculture in the state.
And whereas in 2025, Representative Zamora announced his candidacy for New Mexico's third congressional district.
And his campaign will focus on economic opportunity, energy independence, border security and protecting the agricultural backbone of northern New Mexico.

Now therefore be it resolved by the House of Representatives of the state of New Mexico that Representative Martin R Zamora be honored and recognized for his outstanding leadership and service to the residents of House District 63 and New Mexico.

It's great for the state of New Mexico. We are staying in neutering 5,000 animals, cats and dogs every year, and we should continue doing that and allow the court to decide what the court decides.

Mr. Speaker, and gentlemen, you know, what we decide today will, the courts will, if what we're deciding today is to remove the sunset, to keep the program.
