Thank you, Mr. Speaker.
Good afternoon.
Thanks for getting me there.
Good afternoon, members.
This afternoon I have the pleasure and honor of presenting the Senate Finance Committee.
Committee substitute for Senate Bill 151, which is also known as the tax package.
And so this 2026 tax package may be the first year that the actual tax package originates originated in the Senate.
Usually this process takes place initially in the house and then we do our work in our own committee respective committee and then we send over.
Our package over then to the Senate for their inclusion of their own amendments.

However, because of the circumstances around a certain piece of legislation that would essentially be the vehicle in garnering the capacity to allow us to have a tax package originating in the Senate,
it was just thought that perhaps that's probably the better vehicle to use to get us to this point they were at today.
So the Senate sent over its package that both raised capacity and targeted a handful of Senate priorities with tax cuts.
Those priorities that the Senate sent over and I'll go over these in more detail as the debate goes on today.
But generally speaking, those Senate priorities included health care practitioner with doctor incentives, local journalism incentives, and affordable housing incentives as well.
In a similar fashion, in a similar fashion, when we pass out this tax package from the house side, we ensure that we allow for at least half of the capacity to be utilized by the Senate.
And our Senate friends on their side, so in return this year was just the same.
They ensure that we had half of their capacity, which in this case the vehicle Senate Bill 151 raises about $110 million per year.
And so that being said, they retain for us and for our uses at around $60 million.
The following discussion really provides context and insight to those Senate priorities, then we then I'll give us a little bit of a preview of our house amendments to that package.
And then we can then we can debate a little bit more.
So without further ado, Mr. Speaker, I'd like to be able to talk a little bit more about the federal corporate income tax decoupling, which is again, as I mentioned, Senate Bill 151,
which is, again, our vehicle to ensure that we would have the capacity raised to provide these tax incentives or these incentives period for the people of New Mexico.
Let me be clear in this discussion as we begin that House Bill or Senate Bill 151, the corporate income tax decoupling, this is not a new tax.
Nor is this a tax increase. Let me repeat that for the people in the back.
This is not a new tax nor is this a tax increase.

The vehicle by which Senate Bill 151, the federal corporate income tax decoupling, does is that it simply decouples and returns New Mexico back to before the passage of the federal law house resolution.
Number one, that was passed in July of 2025.
In July of 2025, if you all recall, the federal government passed huge sweeping tax breaks that mostly benefited large businesses and have automatically flowed through and two New Mexico's tax code.

Now, at this point, Mr. Speaker and members, New Mexico, we don't have the luxury, we don't have the federal government's ability to give billions and tax breaks and tax cuts and go into debt and not be responsible with our own financial resources and prudent responsibility for our own citizens in our state.
We have to have a balanced budget.

In August, for example, Mr. Speaker and members, since the passage of that federal house resolution number one, the forecast through our legislative finance committee, and I'd be remiss if I didn't recognize them right now, is that with me today we have our chief economist, Ishmael Torres, and we have Jennifer Fabian, who is also with our legislative finance committee that will help us out.
And I think I also want to recognize Amanda Brideling, who is sitting there next to our chairman, Small, who will help us out if we have any other specific questions.
But in August, in our legislative finance committee hearings, it was forecasted that through corporate income tax, we would expect to have revenues of new money in the area of about $400 million.
We were expected to raise $400 million before house resolution number one was passed.
Then it was passed, and that changed the forecast dramatically.
It changed it to where it was once $400 million raised for the benefit of New Mexicans to then downwards to just barely over $100 million.

Why such a decrease? It's because, as I made mentioned before, the passage of federal house resolution number one was to benefit large businesses, multinational corporations, and automatically impact the citizens and the people of New Mexico.
That was again.
Primarily due to those corporate tax changes again at that federal level.
What Senate Bill 151 does is that it actually un-links New Mexico from the two costliest federal changes and links to one federal policy governing the taxation of foreign corporate income.
Let me repeat that again.
This bill un-links New Mexico from the two costliest federal changes and links to one federal policy that governs the taxation of foreign corporate income.
In all 50 states, they have selectively decoupled from portions of the federal tax code.
This is not a novel concept that we are trying to do in New Mexico.
This doesn't set us apart. We're not an outlier. In all 50 states, they have selectively decoupled themselves from portions of the federal tax code.
The decoupling provisions in this bill are particularly popular amongst the states for decoupling because they are so costly to each state's bottom line.
New Mexico is not any different than those other states.
Let me be clear though, this bill does not decouple every provision.
It leaves in place some targeted tax policies like providing incentives for research and development.
Companies and small businesses. It leaves those in place.
The three areas by which this bill decouples from the federal tax code are these.
First, first year bonus depreciation. New Mexico will continue with accelerated depreciations that existed once again before House Resolution No. 1.
We've had this and the Mexico was electing to continue to have it and not given to the pressures of an administration federally.
Secondly, we will elect to, in our first year, first year expensing for manufacturing facilities.
Again, continuing with the previous tax structure that we've enjoyed in New Mexico.
Lastly, expanded business deductions for interest.
Once again, returning to the status quo that was enjoyed in New Mexico.
Once again, for the people in the back, this is not a new tax on New Mexicans.
This is not an increase in tax for New Mexicans.
I know on social media it may be popular to talk about Senate Bill 151 is coming after you or they're going to raise your costs of living or they're going to do something it really doesn't.
This is the same structure that we've enjoyed for decades and through the passage of House Resolution No. 1 on the federal level.

It's going to cost our state trillions of dollars by which we are not willing to and have no interest in wanting to share those and allow those corporate profits just to go to the pockets of those billionaires outside of New Mexico.
We want to enjoy those benefits here in New Mexico.
At this point, it should also be mentioned that, and I've made mention to it before, New Mexico is not an outlier in decoupling.

We are not. As a matter of fact, it should be made mention that there are 26 states in the United States that have already decoupled and chosen to decouple from the first year bonus depreciation and many other states across our country are further considering decoupling.
Like we are here maintaining accelerated depreciation. As a matter of fact, if we are successful today, we may be the 27th.
And a little bit closer to home to give you a little bit of context, one of those 26 today is our neighbor to the west, the state of Arizona.

So if the argument wants to be made that we are taking ourselves out of contention to be able to attract new businesses or retain businesses that exist here in New Mexico, there could be nothing farther from the fact that as a matter of fact, we are simply just protecting ourselves our interests and our citizens.
Furthermore, 11 states in our nation are decoupling from first year expenses for manufacturing. 12 states in our nation are decoupled from the additional expansion of business deductions.

And lastly, this bill, with all it does, it conforms with the federal government's attempt to sweep in all of the monies from the United States companies that have established foreign subsidiaries overseas and pushed assets and cash to lower tax areas, that's been a problem for the federal government for decades.
Let's stash our money overseas and somewhere in some place that we don't know where it's at.
We are conforming with the latest efforts to add those back to our tax base, as well as already 22 other states in our nation currently do.
This, again, ladies and gentlemen, Mr. Speaker, this vehicle continues the status quo that we have enjoyed for decades.
This continues the status quo that we have enjoyed for decades.
It pushes back on wanting to put the dollars back into corporate profits and it maintains that the dollars that are raised in New Mexico stay in New Mexico.
As a matter of fact, the vehicle in Senate Bill 151 raises more than $110 million once fully implemented.
And future proofs us, excuse me, from future changes in these sections unless we agree to them in New Mexico.

That's Senate Bill 151 and the vehicle by which we are going to raise money to ensure that we can have the capacity to enjoy these additions and these incentives that the Senate brought over into our attention in our committee this morning.
Again, Senate Bill 151 includes those provisions by which they elected to include as tax incentives into this tax omnimous bill.
I will go over those details right now.
In sections 1 and 2, excuse me, yep, in sections 1 and 2, it covers, again, those things that I mentioned earlier in my presentation.
It decouples us from those federal tax policies.

Section 3 and 4, what they elected to do in the Senate and send over our way is the appreciation for what is known as the local journalist refundable credit, local news wages, that is Senate Bill 120.
What that does, it creates a refundable tax credit for local news organizations equal to 30% of annual wages.
Furthermore, it's designed to support local journalism capacity by offsetting payroll costs and incentivizing the employment or retention of qualified journalists.
Furthermore, the statewide annual cap of $4 million total credit per year is attached to this part of their tax package.
Which it would apply beginning in 2027 and sunset in 2032.
The next portion that the Senate sent over as a part of its tax package to provide incentives or tax credits or tax incentives to the Mexicans is 1 in section 5.
And that is titled the Affordable Manufacturing Multi-Family Housing Construction GRT deduction.
That is under Senate Bill 92.
What Senate Bill 92 does is it creates a GRT deduction for the construction materials and labor used in affordable multi-family housing projects.
The intention of this bill is to reduce project costs for qualifying affordable multi-family developments, improving viability to leverage public and private housing resources.
This again will go effective and go live in July of 2027 with the sunset of 2030.
Next, what the Senate sent over for its inclusion into the tax packages here is section 6.
Section 6 includes physician income tax credit, Senate Bill 12.
What Senate Bill 12 does is that it creates a $10,000 personal income tax credit for qualified physicians.
The criteria being that those physicians must be licensed in New Mexico, providing at least 1,584 hours of health care service to our state for our citizens per year.
And eligibility is certified by the Department of Health.
This, in fact, ladies and gentlemen and Mr. Speaker, will be the biggest physician personal income tax incentive in the country.
We talked a lot this year, Mr. Speaker, and members of this body about how do we fix the doctor's shortage in New Mexico?
How do we fix health care in New Mexico?
There have been a number of provisions that have passed out of this house, and I think that we are all proud of the work that all of us have done in the very bipartisan nature.
This adds to that collective.
This adds to that collective that unlike complicated GRT deductions that have impacts on local government, this credit for the doctors will offset GRT.
Payed by physicians with no local governmental impact at all.
Simple and easy to apply for.
This would go into effect in January of 2027, and with sunset in January of 2032.
What was also added in the Senate provisions to their tax package was Section 7.
Noted as the Health Care Equipment GRT deduction, Senate Bill 133.

What Senate Bill 133 does, or was supposed to do, and I'm reading you on to something that we did, is that it was supposed to create a new deduction on selling certain in-office equipment and non-prescription in-office medications
to health care providers.
Now, in our committee, as a part of our tax package, we struck that provision.
We struck that provision for one purpose that was pretty well talked about in many circumstances, and that was the effect that that GRT would have on our local municipalities.
We heard loud and clear from those local governments, and we were not willing to put them in a compromising position for this bill.
So it takes them out of the risk of losing that GRT capacity that they enjoyed today.
Finally, in Section 7 and 8, the Senate sent over this provision called the local news printer credit, Senate Bill 150.
What Senate Bill 150 does is it creates a refundable tax credit for businesses that provides for manufacturing, production, and printing services for a local news organization.
It's a credit of up to $10,000 for each employee employed by the news printer.
And just as the others have had a sunset, this one does, too.
It will begin in 2027, and it will sunset in 2032.
That is the Senate's tax package.
That's the vehicle by which the capacity is raised and those provisions by which they are electing and proposing the capacity is spent on to help New Mexicans all across our state.
Coming now to the work that our House Taxation and Revenue Committee did this past session.
We heard numerous bills as we all do in our respective committees, all with great merit, all with great incentives, all with fantastic ideas.
And for those that were not selected to be in this year's tax package, I would encourage those sponsors to come back.
Because those ideas that you have, those people that you represented are very much worth it.
We want to see them back because we believe that they had absolutely excellent merit.
However, we are bound by a certain dollar capacity.
And in being responsible, we cannot go over that dollar capacity.
So now knowing that in the House Taxation and Revenue Committee, we have about $60 million to build our side of the tax package.

What do you do with $60 million where you can create good tax policy, where you can do something that's good for everybody, where you can do something that you can hang your hat on and feel like you've done something great for the people of New Mexico.
Well, what we did this year for the House Amendments to the Senate Tax Package is in Section 9.
What Section 9 does and what it's titled is the high wage job tax credits that is under House Bill 145.
And I want to recognize one of our minority committee members, the gentleman from Sandeval County, Representative Hernandez, that brought this to our attention.
What the high wage job tax credit does is it extends the sunset for the high wage job tax credit from something that's going to expire July 1 of this very year.
And it extends it all the way to July 1 of 2036.
At this point, what this high wage job tax credit does is that employers that create high wage jobs in New Mexico may apply for a tax credit against state GRT.
Compensating tax, withholding tax, the interstate telecommunications tax, GRT, E911 surcharges, and telecommunication relay services surcharges.
And as an eligible employer, the employer is eligible for JTIP administered by the Economic Development Department.
And so at this point in time, the total cost that we would expand from the House's side of the capacity is between $5 and $6 million of the 60 that I referenced earlier.
Now where this gets a little bit different, I will say it in that way, is that in this body, when we don't have enough capacity in our committee,
we don't have enough capacity to do something groundbreaking.
Something that we can say that gosh, we are changing the landscape for tax policy in our state because those are big spends.
Those things often cost $100 million plus if we intend to work the way that it should work.
So if you recall in this body, in this chamber about two weeks ago, we heard a bill called House Bill 2.
In House Bill 2, there was a provision in there that afforded a 1% compensation increase for all of our public employees.
That was something that the Appropriations Committee voted on proudly in a bipartisan manner as I understand it.
That was something that this body stood behind in full force in a bipartisan supportive manner that we passed.
We passed in this House, sent it over to the Senate, and it's not our place to question the rationale, but for some reason that 1% compensation increase was removed.
It was removed.
And when it's removed and our people are left out in the cold with their pockets not as full as they could be, we have to do something to make it right.
So instead of trying to scramble to create a tax package with a bunch of Christmas tree or Christmas ornaments to decorate that tree and try to make something out of nothing,
what our committee elected to do, what our staff was able to help us collaborate with, was that we thought of, well, why don't we take care of the people?
Why don't we take care of the people of New Mexico? Why don't we take care of those public employees? Let's take care of those school teachers.
Let's take care of those university employees. Let's take care of the janners and the people that work in this very building.
Let's take care of those police officers and those firefighters and everyone else that may be a public employee in this state.

And let's do what is the right thing to do, not politically motivated, not pushed by somebody else, but sometimes it's okay in this building to do something that doesn't have to score political points.
And that's what this bill does. That's what our provision to reinstate the 1% compensation back into this legislature for consideration by the governor does is that it uses the capacity,
allowed it to the house in our tax package to fully fund a 1% salary increase for public employees that include, again, state agencies,
higher education institutions and public schools.
This matches the compensation plan, again, as I made mentioned before, that was included in the house past budget before, again, it was unfortunately stripped by the Senate.
We're going to make it right.
In addition to that, I made mention of House Bill 145, the Highway's Jobs Tax Credit.

I also made mention, again, of what we did to strip out the GRT deduction on medical equipment and supplies and response, again, to the concerns that we heard from local governments on the impacts to their revenues.
What we also did is that we arranged dates. This was a provision that came over from the Senate that postponed the House's ability to enact its spend until 2028, so they pushed this out a year.
But because of the urgency for these 1% compensation raises to become law, because of the urgency that this Highway's Jobs Tax Credit is,
because it expires on July 1st of this very year, we reversed that.
So we now push that, and we amended in the bill, that the House's spend begin this next fiscal year in FY2027, and that the Senate is pushed back to FY2028.

And finally, Mr. Speaker and general men and general lady of the House, what our amendments do is that it ensures that sunset dates passed by the Senate were kept and it also adds sunsets to sections where they previously did not exist in keeping with best practices for evaluating tax expenditures.
And so with all that said, Mr. Speaker, we believe in our committee that we have done the right thing in our amendment.
We can argue about the merits of, well, we should raise the money differently, or maybe we have money in the bank or some other thing.
But at the end of the day, I'll go back to the first thing I said when I started this presentation.
The vehicle used in Senate Bill 151. It is not a new tax. It's not an expansion of a tax.
It is the status quo of what was enjoyed prior to July of 2025 when the federal government elected to pass House Resolution No.1.
We are using our sovereign status as a state. We are selecting to maintain that we are going to protect the Mexico and the Mexicans the right way.
We are simply doing what other states have already done.
We may, if this becomes law, become the 27th state in the nation to do this.
And for those of us that are here that are concerned that it takes us out of the running to attract new businesses or retain those that are currently here, it doesn't.
Because at this point, New Mexico is a great place to live. We should all be proud to represent New Mexico. And with that, Mr. Speaker, I will stand for any questions.
There is a debate. Let's go first to the general lady from Don Yana and Otero Representative Silva.
Thank you, Mr. Speaker. Does the gentleman yield?
Yes, Mr. Speaker. I yield to my friend from Don Yana County.
Thank you, Mr. Speaker and gentlemen. I want to thank you for bringing this bill to our committee and for the Senate for drafting half of it in our conversation this morning.
I want to talk a little bit about the investments. I know you spoke in your opening about the investments in terms of our tax investments.
Can you talk a little bit about the people investments when we look at our the House edition of the 1% increase for state workers in their pay?
Can you talk a little bit about the people investments that we're making here?
Mr. Speaker and gentlemen, thank you for that question. The people power and the people in the Mexico is why we're all here, right?
They are the backbone of our culture, of our diversity, of our economy, of everything that we are as a state, right?
And when you talk about the people power, we lead with that first, I believe, in this chamber.
We want to make sure that we take care of the people first. And so at this point in time, when you talk about the people and you talk about the raises, maybe raises is a strong word.
1% is not a lot of money. And I think we all recognize that. It's a stark, but it's not a finish.
So when we talk about the 1% compensation increase, that is putting money back into the people's pockets of New Mexico.

That means that those people that are living in Chaparral, or in Ratón, or San Juan, or Gallup, and all across our state, that if they can have, and they are a public employee, and they are making ends meet, that could mean an extra $50 a month, $100 a month, for some people in terms of people power.

That means they don't have to worry about where dinner is coming from for the month. That means that cell phones don't get turned off so that in case an emergency happens, they don't have to worry and rush to try to figure it out.
How am I going to call emergency? They don't have to, they can be relieved of maybe ensuring that, yes, the rent is going to be paid on time or the mortgage or car payment will be paid on time.

When you talk about that, that's the people power of New Mexico, making sure that our own have what they need, not just to survive, but to thrive and to be able to enjoy a lifestyle if they are in this case a public employee.
It also makes sure Mr. Speaker and gentle lady, I'm sorry if I'm being long winded, but I think I want to make sure that we cover this.
Mr. Speaker, gentle lady, that when we talk about people power, we know that many of our people that work in public employee positions in our state, they come from small towns.
Not everybody lives in Albuquerque, Santa Fe, Las Cruces, or those other larger municipalities, right?
Some of them come from the shopperels, some of them come from the rat tones, some of them come from the cubas of our state.

And when they do, they can make that money and take it back to those local grocers, to those local businesses that are in those shopperels that are in those rat tones, or that are in those cubas in our state.
At this point in time, it is absolutely fundamental that we know that the people power is what we led with.
It is absolutely fundamental to know that it's the people power that will ensure that we are doing the right thing.

And it's for those recent Mr. Speaker and gentle lady that when we talk about people power, you strike the target exactly on the bulls eye because you know people power, and that's what we always lead with.
Thank you, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen, I appreciate that.

And would you also say that in our committee, we talk a lot about putting money directly into people's pockets versus having them have to apply for a tax credit or a rebate that can often slow down getting money back into the pockets of people.
And this is the type of policy that is a direct infusion. Can you speak a little bit to those conversations we've had in your point of view on that Mr. Speaker, gentlemen?
Mr. Speaker and generally that's a great point.
You know, when we talk about in not allowing for handouts, I think people mistake people saying, we're just giving out handouts, these are handouts.

When we're encouraging people to go to work, and we can pay them a fair wage for their daily work, their daily hourly work, that that means that those people that are getting up at 6 a.m. going to work by eight coming home and getting their family fed and back to bed, that means that they're going to do so because it's encouraging they're getting compensated well.
Yes, we have social programs, Mr. Speaker and General Lady, that will help those that may not be able to work.
That's not what we're talking about here. What we're talking about here is our ability to help those that have gainful employment as a public employee here.

And Mr. Speaker and General Lady, if you recall in our committee hearings, we have those really engaging discussions about encouraging and enticing and uplifting those individuals through like an expansion of a working families tax credit,
or working tax credit of sorts, that engages people, that ensures that for every dollar more that they make, they can receive a little bit better of a benefit.

Because if the person is struggling and they're having a hard time getting by, if the institutions and their community are strong, then that's going to strengthen them. And vice versa, if people are strong and the communities are strong, they are there for also strengthening their institutions.

Mr. Speaker and General Lady, so in regards to all of those that you made mention of before, the investments that are made and to provide context are really just, it provides the capacity that Mr. Speaker and General Lady, that oftentimes is lacking in any state, right?

You know, it further goes on in regards to those that are perhaps local journalists or have those local newspapers in their communities, right?
To make sure that some people, Mr. Speaker and General Lady, when they grow up and they want to be a journalist, they want to report on their hometown because that's where they come from, right?
You represent where you're born, where you're raised, and where you go back to, and call home, right?

So Mr. Speaker and General Lady, when you can invest in the people that work there, that thrive there, that want to live there, that's a special thing.

When you want to invest in their businesses to make sure that they have the capacity, again, using that word to ensure that they can retain those credits for their ability to buy the printer, to buy the computers, to buy the necessities they need to produce a working paper.

But Mr. Speaker and General, if you're going back to the capacity and the people power and injecting and creating that capacity, we want to make sure that we can do that.

And so whether it's housing, whether it's that local journalist aspect, or whether it's something that we've identified in this very session, recognizing it's a problem, and that's the doctor's shortage, or it's a healthcare problem, right?
When you don't have those people that will help us remain to be, or help us to be a healthy state, it's difficult.

The fact of the matter is that in Mexico is showing that it cares about their livelihood, it cares about their profession.

And so it's that people power and that injection of dollars to create the capacity and very pinpointed and identified ways that will help create a better New Mexico tomorrow, Mr. Speaker and General Lee.
Thank you, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen. I appreciate that.

That that creates cohesion. The same thing with healthcare as we're building up and reinforcing our healthcare institutions.

Mr. Speaker in general, you stated eloquently, beautifully, and you hit on the point that I think that I was trying to make, but you say so much better, that when you grow up in the community, and I'll give you Mr. Speaker in general, maybe a little snapshot of where I come from.

Mr. Speaker, general lady, you identified several of those that we've done over the past year at this point and so this is absolutely without a doubt my mind, Mr. Speaker and general lady, a continuation of the great work that all of us in this body, maybe I'm being overzealous and saying that the great work, but we should all be able to pat ourselves in the back because we are responding to certain situations and circumstances that are affecting New Mexico today.

And then second with our schools and with journalism as we've seen on the national level, these huge newspapers getting taken over by billionaires, we want to really invest in local voices and local journalism.

Mr. Speaker and General Lady as much as I like to continue to talk about where we come from and places we call home and continue to brag upon the things that we believe that we are doing proactively to help in the Mexico.

It's got to obviously align with our values as New Mexicans and as New Mexico and also align with our economic conditions in our state.

Mr. Speaker and General Lady, I often watch the reaction from our esteemed appropriations, share the gentlemen from Donia Anna before I react to myself.

But upon my initial reaction, I was stunned and appalled and felt really unfortunate for the people in New Mexico knowing that that extra capacity in this case going from 400 million to just a little over 100 million was going to be taken away from the people in New Mexico and the things that we could utilize that for.
In this case, those 1% wage increases was really unfortunate knowing that it was going to go to those large multi national corporations for their own likely benefit and profit.

Hiring had slowed across our country to Mexico, not being an exception. The job market is harder to find a job in any one location and not just in our state, but all across our country and Mr. Speaker and General Lady.

Thank you, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen. So again, back to that 1% cost of living adjustment that our New Mexicans, not only, you know, families and individuals, but they are seeing their own economic headwinds as we are facing a state that our families are also facing those same headwinds.

So when we look at that that cut from 400 million to a little, a little over 100 million, where we consulted was the state consulted by our federal government about this shift as they were debating HR one, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen.
Mr. Speaker and and General Lady, no, we were not.
So Mr. Speaker, gentlemen, when we are not consulted and the government, the federal government makes all of these economic changes very quickly, very rapidly.
We look at tariffs. We look at I would even say the decrease in in migration that impacts economic and workforce and our workforce in New Mexico that all of those things compounded.
So where does that put us as a state? I know that there's a number of states in our country that are now facing recession like environment.
What's it like here in New Mexico? Are we in a recession? Are we teetering? Are we robust and resilient? What's the?
Senator, Mr. Speaker?
State your point. Mr. Speaker, I don't think that this conversation has anything to do other states, what they've done with recession, language like that. We need to stick to the bill.

Mr. Speaker, gentlemen, so can you talk a little bit about what New Mexico is facing in terms of its own economic headwinds? Are we resilient? Are we trading water?
Mr. Speaker, gentlemen.

Regarding your question, General Lady, from Donia and I County, no state is recession-proof, right?

But luckily enough for New Mexico, Mr. Speaker and General Lady, that over the past four to six years I imagine that this body, this legislature, with really being led by the professionals in the legislative finance committee, have helped us to create a sense of stability
that once was this feast or famine mode that we have been trapped in for years, right?
And so today, to your point, Mr. Speaker and General Lady, as it respects to your question, New Mexico today is in a good financial position, especially compared with other states.

That we could risk worsening revenues if in fact a recession takes place.
And so in this case, some of the Mexicans may feel like they are in a recessionary environment today with wages not keeping up.

But in terms of our government as a whole, I think that the things that we have done in regards to building the capacity to create trust funds, to create mechanisms that we cannot have to be at the will and the whim of oil and gas prices.
To dictate whether or not departments or programs continue or that capital outlay continue that we are on a trajectory to be able to sustain and withstand a recession should what happened.
Thank you, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen. I would also credit the work that the House appropriations and tax and rev and other committees and elected leaders here have done.
And the majority have done to create the conditions for us to be more resilient in times of economic headwinds and potential chaos that we're seeing.

Mr. Speaker and General, the absolutely and that's something that as a body, we should all celebrate that we are physically responsible in that way and that all the revenues that we receive in the state aren't just automatically for no reason at all just spent because we have the dollars at hand.

Whether you want to call it creative or bold or imaginative, right? We want to make sure that we're saying that it's just the right thing to do.
It's the prudent thing to do as physical agents or as agents of our state here as legislatures in this body.

And so that we can still become and be and retain ourselves as a player nationally and globally.

Thank you, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen. And so thinking about our creativity and looking at how others have also done this in the last year is that we are choosing to decouple our corporate income tax from conforming with the federal tax code. Is that correct, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen?

Mr. Speaker and gentle lady know but imagine the world that that would be if we could.

Would you agree with that, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen? Mr. Speaker and gentlemen, not only did I agree, but I overwhelmingly agree and I think of examples like, you know, when we talk about our ability to provide a 1% increase our compensation to our state employees.

So when we can provide this type of balancing act, if you will, to provide an economic relief, if you will, if you want to call it something else, that we are doing things in a proactive nature to help all of those in our state, especially in this case, those on a more rural level.
Thank you, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen. And when we talk about decoupling, and I know I hope it's okay that I do talk about our neighboring states.
Because this is, I think, the policy that we're creating does impact our ability to attract businesses to retain businesses and that we're competing with neighboring states.
And so when we look at the decoupling and looking at, and I want to look particularly at full expensing versus bonus depreciation, is there research about which one is better for a state?
Yeah, is there like empirical evidence that like this way is way better than this other way of doing it? Mr. Speaker, gentlemen.

Mr. Speaker and gentlemen, a very interesting question. And so when we think about is one better than the other, there's really not a consensus in research that there are only certain folks on either side that may argue one or the other but ultimately state or state corporate income taxes are not a large share.
Of corporate expenditures. And so that would be the answer to your question.

Thank you, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen, because I know that the federal government is moving more towards full expensing and that we are sticking closer to bonus depreciation. Is that correct, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen?

Mr. Speaker and gentle lady actually know where we're actually sticking with accelerated expenses and so that's that's in our case, the reasoning why the sponsor, at least on the Senate side, put that in this legislation based on the professional recommendations obviously of our economists that help craft this bill.
Thank you, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen, and can you explain accelerated depreciation just in the nutshell for me again, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen?
Sure, absolutely. So Mr. Speaker and generally accelerated depreciation is when the depreciation is based off of the useful life of a property rather than 100% of the cost of the of the first year.
Thank you, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen. So if I can repeat back and correct me if I'm wrong.
So so full expenses I can write off as a business owner. I can write off the full expense at the front ends and take that as a deduction. Is that correct, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen?
Mr. Speaker and gentle lady, yes, ma'am.

And I can take a percentage of that every year until it loses until it's at zero value, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen.

Mr. Speaker and generally you're you're right on. So yes, is the answer to your question with a little bit of explanation and usually with a little bit of a bigger share in the first year like like 40%.
Thank you, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen, and and is why would the federal government stay at 100% of full expense? Why is that choice being made, Mr. Speaker, gentlemen?

Well, Mr. Speaker and general lady, I think there's a number of thoughts behind this, but really I think the intent is to encourage investment and to encourage spending by increasing the deduction against against one's taxes.
